coredata.dc:title: Controllable construction of ultrathin graphene quantum dots/polyamide nanofilms via electrospray interfacial polymerization
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Yumin
Liu, Hailiang
Qin, Yang
Feng, Xianshe
Chen, Junyan
Huang, Qinglin
Xiao, Changfa
coredata.dc:description: Ultrathin polyamide (PA) nanofilms with a controllable structure are of great significance in nanofiltration (NF). We report on a novel approach to preparing PA nanofilms based on electrospray and interfacial polymerization (E-IP) by incorporating graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in piperazine (PIP) solution and co-reacting with 1,3,5-benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC) solution on the surface of a polyvinyl chloride (PVC) substrate membrane. The physicochemical structures of the PA nanofilms (like the crosslinking degree, zeta potential, pore size distribution, surface roughness and the thickness) were tailored by adjusting the electrospray time and GQDs concentrations. The results showed that the addition of GQDs significantly reduced the electrospray time required for the resulting membrane to achieve a Na2SO4 rejection of over 95 %, with a significantly reduced skin layer thickness in the PA nanofilms. Compared to the PA/PVC NF membrane without the addition of GQDs, the GQDs-PA/PVC NF membrane formed at an electrospray time of 15 min exhibited enhanced separation performance, with the Na2SO4 rejection increasing from 75.0 % to 98.8 % and water flux increasing from 34.5 to 41.0 L/(m2·h). In addition, the NF membranes formed at short electrospray times (i.e., 2, 4, and 5 min) could achieve more than 98 % Congo Red (CR) rejection and exhibit a minor increase in permeation flux after the addition of GQDs. This study provided an insight into the uniform addition of nanomaterials into PA nanofilms to fine-tune the membrane structure.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrospray and interfacial polymerization
Ultrathin polyamide nanofilm
Controllable structure
Graphene quantum dots
Nanofiltration

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the PVC substrate membrane
Characterization of GQDs and GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes
Surface properties of GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the research funding provided by the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by China Association for Science and Technology (YESS20200231), the financial support from the China Scholarship Council (202008120087). We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University for the test analysis work.

The authors gratefully acknowledge the research funding provided by the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by China Association for Science and Technology (YESS20200231), the financial support from the China Scholarship Council (202008120087). We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University for the test analysis work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the electrospray process of the GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes.

Characteristics of the GQDs: (a) Chemical structure; (b) FTIR spectra; (c) TEM image (inset: size distribution of GQDs, with an average size about 3.31 nm); (d) XPS survey spectra elemental composition of the GQDs; (e) XPS spectra of O 1 s; (f) XPS spectra of C 1 s.

(a) Schematic of the chemical interaction between PIP and GQDs in aqueous phase, and reaction between GQDs and TMC; (b) TEM image of GQDs in GQDs-PA/PVC NF membrane dense layer (M0.5-40); (c) and (d) FTIR spectra of PA/PVC NF and GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes.
(a) TEM image of GQDs in GQDs-PA/PVC NF membrane dense layer; (b) and (c) the surface EDS analysis of GQDs-PA/PVC NF membrane (M0.05-15, M0.1-15, M0.5-15).

(a) and (b) XPS spectra of PA/PVC NF and GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes; (c) O/N ratio and crosslinking degree of PA/PVC NF and GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes; (d) XPS spectra of C 1 s of GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes.
The properties of PA/PVC and GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes: (a) pore size and pore size distribution; (b) and (c) Zeta potential; (d) and (e) water contact angle.
(a) Cross-sectional morphologies; (b) relationship between PA nanofilm thickness and electrospray time of PA/PVC NF membranes.
Surface morphologies of PA/PVC NF membranes with varies electrospray time.
Surface and cross-sectional morphologies of GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes with varies GQDs concentration.

Salt and dye rejection tests of the NF membranes: (a), (c) and (d) Na2SO4 rejection and permeation flux; (b) salt rejection of Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2 and NaCl; (e) permeation flux (CR/ Na2SO4 mixed solution); (f) rejection of CR and Na2SO4.
(a) and (b) Short-term stability of M0.1-15 and M0.5-15 for Na2SO4 rejection and permeation flux.
(a) and (b) Circulating dye desalination test of M0.1-2 and M0.1-4.

(a) Comparison of the NaCl/Na2SO4 separation performance in GQDs-PA/PVC NF membrane with various E-IP membranes in literature [18,19,32,33,48,52,53,65]; (b) comparison of the dye desalination performance in GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes with various membranes in literature [17,54–62] (details are given in Table S1 and Table S2).
The code of the NF membranes under different conditions.

head.ce:title: Controllable construction of ultrathin graphene quantum dots/polyamide nanofilms via electrospray interfacial polymerization

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes/National Center for International Joint Research on Separation Membranes
School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrathin polyamide (PA) nanofilms with a controllable structure are of great significance in nanofiltration (NF). We report on a novel approach to preparing PA nanofilms based on electrospray and interfacial polymerization (E-IP) by incorporating graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in piperazine (PIP) solution and co-reacting with 1,3,5-benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC) solution on the surface of a polyvinyl chloride (PVC) substrate membrane. The physicochemical structures of the PA nanofilms (like the crosslinking degree, zeta potential, pore size distribution, surface roughness and the thickness) were tailored by adjusting the electrospray time and GQDs concentrations. The results showed that the addition of GQDs significantly reduced the electrospray time required for the resulting membrane to achieve a Na2SO4 rejection of over 95 %, with a significantly reduced skin layer thickness in the PA nanofilms. Compared to the PA/PVC NF membrane without the addition of GQDs, the GQDs-PA/PVC NF membrane formed at an electrospray time of 15 min exhibited enhanced separation performance, with the Na2SO4 rejection increasing from 75.0 % to 98.8 % and water flux increasing from 34.5 to 41.0 L/(m2·h). In addition, the NF membranes formed at short electrospray times (i.e., 2, 4, and 5 min) could achieve more than 98 % Congo Red (CR) rejection and exhibit a minor increase in permeation flux after the addition of GQDs. This study provided an insight into the uniform addition of nanomaterials into PA nanofilms to fine-tune the membrane structure.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Polyamide-based nanofiltration (NF) membranes, which are widely used for desalination and wastewater reclamation, are a promising technology for resolving the problem of water shortage [1,2]. Preparing a suitable polyamide (PA) nanofilm as the dense layer of a nanofiltration (NF) membrane is critical to the membrane performance. At present, interfacial polymerization (IP) is the most common method to prepare the dense layers of NF membranes [3,4]. Conventionally, IP is carried out by immersing the substrate membrane into an aqueous solution of a monomer, followed by contacting the second monomer in an oil-phase, thereby inducing a reaction between the two monomers at the interface [5,6]. However, the fast reaction rate makes it challenging to obtain controllable skin layer thickness with a loose structure appropriate for nanofiltration. Therefore, many attempts have been made to improve the IP process or develop alternative IP methods for preparing ultrathin PA nanofilms in the past decade [7-12].

Current methods for adjusting the PA nanofilms structure mainly include interface modulation [13], controlling of the aqueous reactant conditions [14], free-standing IP [15], and 3D printing and electrospray [16,17]. In which, electrospray interfacial polymerization (E-IP) is a novel, eco-friendly and handy method for preparing PA nanofilms [16]. Under a high-voltage static electrical gradient, the two reactant solutions were dispersed via their respective metering pump needles into microdroplets, which were deposited simultaneously onto a substrate surface for polymerization to form a PA nanofilm. The thickness of the PA layer can be controlled by adjusting the various parameters involved in the electrospray process, and the reaction rate between the two monomers can also be adjusted indirectly to obtain proper microporous structures in the PA nanofilm, thus the E-IP method is suited for constructing loose NF membranes with an ultrathin PA layer, high dye/salt selectivity and smooth surface [18–23]. Though such PA NF membranes work well for rejection of organic dyes, effective rejection of divalent salts with a desired permeation flux is difficult to achieve [24].

The selection of appropriate nanomaterial additives is a crucial factor for regulating the structure of the PA nanofilm. Graphene quantum dots (lateral dimensions ≤ 10 nm), a derivative of graphene, have been widely used as nano-additives to modify PA nanofilm structure and generate an optimum for the application of separation and permeation because of their excellent physical/chemical properties (e.g., nano size, good dispersion in water, frictionless surface, chemical stability, excellent dispersion into the polymer matrix) and abundant edge functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine) [17,25–28]. Li et al. [29] used GQDs as quasi-molecule-scale regulators to reduce the diffusion rate of piperazine (PIP) and yielded loose PA nanofilms with controllable ultrathin thickness in the range of 5.5 to 18.3 nm. Zhang et al. [30] prepared a hybrid thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane with a smoother and more hydrophilic surface by dispersing the GQDs in the tannic acid aqueous solution and co-reacting with isophorone diisocyanate. Compared with the thin film composite (TFC) membrane, this TFN membrane achieved a high dye rejection stability with a water permeance 1.5 times higher. Bi et al. [31] also prepared TFN membranes containing GQDs that showed a high water permeance and excellent anti-fouling performance. However, detailed studies on the adjustment of the microstructures of PA nanofilms by the addition of GQDs in the E-IP process to obtain a series of NF membranes suitable for dye desalination and divalent salt rejection are rather limited [32,33].

In this study, microporous polyvinyl chloride (PVC) membranes, which are advantageous to us due to their low costs and high mechanical strength, were used as the substrate membrane, and ultrathin PA nanofilms with controllable thicknesses and loose structures were prepared on the PVC substrate membrane. GQDs were dispersed into the aqueous PIP solution, followed by reaction with the TMC solution dispersed in an oil phase via a novel E-IP process. The chemical and physical structure of the specific PA nanofilms were tailored by controlling the electrospray time and GQDs concentration in the aqueous phase. The chemical structures, morphologies, surface properties, and separation performance of the as-prepared GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes were investigated.

Polyvinyl chloride resin (PVC, fiber grade, DG-1000 k, average degree of polymerization: 1030) was provided by Tianjin Dagu Chemical Factory (Tianjin, China). N, N-dimethylacetamide (DMAc), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, K-30), polyethylene glycol (PEG, 6000 MW), hexane, Congo Red (CR), NaCl, MgCl2, Na2SO4 and MgSO4 were all obtained from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagents (Tianjin, China). Citric acid (CA) and NaOH were procured from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagents (Tianjin, China). 1,3,5-Benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC) was got from J&K Scientific (Beijing, China). Piperazine (PIP) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China).

The GQDs were synthesized by CA through a bottom-up method [34]. First, an orange liquid was obtained by heating 2 g of CA at 200 ℃ for 30 min, and then NaOH solution at a concentration of 1.0 g/L was used to neutralize the excess CA until pH reached 7, thereby yielding a uniform suspension of the GQDs. The GQDs solution was then dialyzed for 30 h with a membrane having a MWCO of 500 Da, before being distilled under reduced pressure. Finally, the GQDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying the distilled solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the PVC substrate membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The commonly used solution phase inversion method was employed to prepare the PVC substrate membrane. First, a homogeneous casting solution with 12 wt% PVC, 5 wt% PEG, 5 wt% PVP and 78 wt% DMAc was prepared and scraped into a plate shape with a thickness of 250 μm. Then, the plate shape polymer solution was immersed in the water coagulation bath. Finally, the deionized water was used to rinse the obtained PVC substrate membrane for at least 48 h.

Fig. 1 showed the preparation process of the GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes. The PIP aqueous solution (1.5 w/v%) at a flow rate of 3 mL/h and the TMC hexane solution (0.2 w/v%) at a flow rate of 2 mL/hwere discharged as microdroplets. The needle tips were positioned 4 cm (for the aqueous phase) and 3 cm (for the organic phase) from the collector drum, respectively.The PVC substrate membrane was surrounded on the collector drum of the electrospray unit, and the rotation speed of the collector drum was set as 200 rpm.The two monomer solutions were dispersed as microdroplets under the coulombic repulsion forces at the working voltage of 9 kV, which were deposited simultaneously onto the PVC substrate membrane where the IP occurred between the two monomers at their microdroplet interfaces.Membranes (denoted as PA/PVC membranes) were formed at different electrospray time (i.e., 2, 4, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30 and 40 min).

In another set of experiments, membranes were prepared under the same conditions except that the PIP aqueous solution contained different concentrations (0.05, 0.1 and 0.5 g/L) of GQDs, and such membranes were referred to as GQDs-PA/PVC membranes. Finally, the membranes were placed at room temperature for 24 h to get fully cross-linked onto the PVC substrate surface. After rinsing with hexane and water to remove residual monomers, the membranes were stored in deionized water for subsequent testing. Table 1 shows the membrane preparation conditions and the designation of the membrane with and without GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs and GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The prepared GQDs and NF membranes were detected by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher, USA) to identify the surface functional groups. The elemental composition of the samples was analyzed using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, K-alpha, Thermo Fisher, USA). The degrees of crosslinking (D) of the GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes were determined from the atomic concentration of elements detected from the NF membrane sample surface by XPS measurements:
(1)
D
%
=
m
m + n
× 100 %
where m and n represented the cross-linked and the linear portions of the PA nanofilm, respectively. Based on the ratio of oxygen(O) and nitrogen(N), the values of m and n were calculated by Eqs. (2) and (3) [35,36]:
(2)
m + n = 1
(3)
O N
=
3 m + 4 n
3 m + 2 n

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi S4800, Japan) was used to observe the surface and cross-sectional morphologies of the NF membranes. Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, Hitachi H7650, Japan) was used to demonstrate the presence and distribution of GQDs in the PA nanofilm and determine the thickness of the PA layer. Scanning electron microscope-energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS, APOLLO XL, USA) was used to analyze the distribution of elements on the NF membranes surfaces. An atomic force microscope (AFM, Brooker Icon, USA) was applied to characterize the surface roughness of the PA layer. Automated gas sorption analyzer (Autosorb-iQ-C(BET), USA) and solid surface zeta potential analyzer (SURPASS-3, Anton Paar, Austria) were employed to analyze the pore size distribution and the surface charge of the NF membranes, respectively. Contact angle meter (Kruss DSA-100, Germany) was utilized to measure the water contact angle (WCA) of the prepared NF membranes. All the NF membrane samples were subjected to freeze-drying prior to characterizations.

A laboratory cross-flow filtration device was used to evaluate the permeation performance of the NF membranes at room temperature and 0.6 MPa. The NF membranes with an effective area of 7.07 cm2 were pre-pressed with pure water at 0.6 MPa for 1 h to steady the membrane performance before the test. Then, the aqueous solutions of Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2 and NaCl at a salt content of 2.0 g/L were replaced to determine the permeation flux (J) and rejection (R) of the NF membranes by following Eqs. (4) and (5):
(4)
J =
V
A × t
where J was the water permeation flux (L/(m2・h)); V, A and t were the volume of permeated water (L), the membrane effective area (m2) and the permeation time (h), respectively.
(5)
R
( % )
=
( 1 -
C p
C f
)
× 100 %
where Cp and Cf were the permeate and feed concentrations determined by the conductivity meter (OHAUS ST3100C), respectively.

In addition, the same filtration system described above (effective area: 7.07 cm2, pressure: 0.6 MPa) was also used to assess the dye desalination performance of the membranes. Before testing, a mixed solution of CR (0.1 g L-1) and Na2SO4 (2.0 g L-1) was prepared as the feed solution. Then, the membrane permeation flux (J) and rejection (R) were also calculated according to Eqs. (4) and (5). And the UV–Vis spectrophotometer (TU-1810) was used to determine CR concentrations in the permeate and feed solutions.

The NaCl/Na2SO4 selectivity (α1 ) and Na2SO4/CR selectivity (α2 ) were calculated from:
(6)
α 1
=
1 -
R
NaCl
1 -
R
Na
2
SO
4
(7)
α 2
=
1 -
R
Na
2
SO
4
1 -
R
CR
where RNaCl , RNa2SO4 and RCR were the rejections of NaCl, Na2SO4 and CR, respectively. The NaCl/Na2SO4 and Na2SO4/CR selectivity can represent NaCl/Na2SO4 separation performance and dye desalination efficiency of the NF membranes, respectively.

GQDs were prepared by incomplete carbonization of CA and its chemical structure was depicted in Fig. 2
a. FTIR was used to characterize the functional groups of the prepared GQDs, and the result was shown in Fig. 2 b, consistent with those reported previously [37]. The absorption peaks at 1560 and 1390 cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching vibrations of CO. The absorption peaks at 3300 and 1080 cm−1 represented the stretching and bending vibrations of –OH, respectively. And in Fig. 2 d–f, XPS survey spectra exhibited considerable oxygen content (over 40 %), and the typical characteristic peaks of CO and C–O bond were shown in the C 1 s scanning spectra at 288.8 eV and 286.9 eV, respectively. These characteristic peaks further confirmed that the GQDs synthesized contained rich carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [34]. As shown in Fig. 2 c, the GQDs had a narrow size distribution ranging from 1 to 5 nm with an average diameter of 3.31 nm. In addition, under 365 nm UV irradiation, the bright uniform fluorescence indicated that the synthesized GQDs exhibited good water dispersibility ( Fig. S1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface properties of GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
When the GQDs were added to the PIP aqueous phase for membrane formation, the diffusion regulation by means of chemical interactions between the two phases of the monomer solutions was shown in Fig. 3
a [29,38]. The PIP and GQDs were linked by hydrogen bonds and electrostatic attractions owing to hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the edge of GQDs, which could accumulate PIP around the GQDs. And during the E-IP process, GQDs could react with TMC to form the anhydrides and ester groups [39–42]. The TEM images of GQDs-PA/PVC NF membrane (M0.5-40) with a longest electrospray time (40 min) and the highest concentration of GQDs (0.5 g/L) were shown in Fig. 3 b. There were a larger number of black nanoaggregates of GQDs evenly distributed in the PA layer. And the TEM and surface EDS images showed that the distribution of the GQDs nanoaggregates in PA layer was relatively uniform from Fig. 4 . Fig. 3 c, d and
Fig. S2 showed the FTIR spectra of the PA/PVC NF and GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes. In comparison to the PVC substrate membrane, the PA/PVC NF membranes showed additional absorption peaks at 1020 and 1630 cm−1, which was attributed to the C–N and CO stretching vibrations of tertiary amine and carbonyl in the amide groups formed because of the reaction between PIP and TMC [43]. These two peaks confirmed the formation of the PA nanofilms on the PVC substrate by electrospray and interfacial polymerization [31,44]. In additional, there was noticed an extremely broad peak at 3410 cm−1, which was due to the superposition of multiple peaks of –NH and hydroxyl groups, where the –NH stretching vibration was caused by the unreacted residual PIP and the hydroxyl stretching vibration was due to hydrolysis of residual acyl chloride groups on TMC [1,45]. It was also observed that the intensities of these stretching vibration peaks of PA/PVC NF membranes were enhanced with an increase in electrospray time. As shown in Fig. 3 d, for the GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes, the intensities of the vibrational peaks at 1630 and 3410 cm−1 were gradually enhanced with an increase in the GQD content in the aqueous phase of the reactant. The former may be attributed to the superposition of the vibrational peak of CO in GQDs and the vibrational peak of CO in the PA nanofilms [17,39], and the latter was attributed to the introduction of carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups by GQDs [38,46].

Fig. 5 showed the XPS spectra and atomic percentage of PA/PVC and GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes with relation to electrospray time and GQDs concentration. Compared with the PVC substrate ( Fig. S3 ), the C l element was rarely detected in the PA/PVC NF membrane. On the other hand, the elemental contents of N and O were much higher than that of the PVC substrate membrane, indicating the formation of a PA nanofilm on the surface of the PVC substrate membrane [43]. The degree of crosslinking (D) of the PA nanofilm was calculated by Eq.
(1)
, 2). In theory, the O/N ratio for a fully crosslinked structure was 1 (D = 100 %), while the O/N ratio of a linear structure was 2 (D = 0 %) [30]. With an increase in electrospray time from 5 to 40 min, the D value of the PA layer increased from 73.9 % (for membrane M0-5) to 83.8 % (for membrane M0-40) with a reduction in the O/N ratio from 1.2 to 1.1. This was expected because of the increased amounts of the monomers in the two reacting solutions over a longer electrospray time, and as a result a thicker PA nanofilm was formed on the surface of the PVC substrate [47]. In addition, the content of GQDs in theaqueous solution was significantly less than the PIP, thus the D values of GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes were simply calculated using the same approach as described [29]. With an increase in the GQD concentration from 0.05 to 0.5 g/L, the O/N ratio of M-15 decreased from 1.1 to 1.01, while the D value was increased from 88.9 % to 98.6 %, which might indicate that a small loading of GQDs was beneficial to promoting the crosslinking between TMC and PIP. The high resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s of M−15 membranes were shown in Fig. 5 d, where the peaks of OCN at 287.6 eV and C-N at 285.8 eV showed the typical PA structure. Fig. S4 showedthe high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1 s of M−15 membranes, the percentage of O = CN peaks (399.7 eV) were all more than 88 %, indicating that GQDs had no significant blocking effect on the crosslinking reaction between PIP and TMC atthe electrospray time 15 min [48]. These results demonstrated that the polymerization between TMC and PIP occurred and formed the PA nanofilm during the electrospray procedure. The degree of crosslinking of the GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes (e.g., M0.1-15 and M0.5-15) was improved by the addition of a small quantity of GQDs in the PIP solution during membrane formation.

In order to study the effects of electrospray time and GQDs concentrations on the structure and properties of the resulting GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes, these membranes were subjected to characterizations for pore size distribution, zeta potential and water contact angle, and the test results were shown in Fig. 6 . As demonstrated in Fig. 6 a, the pore size distributions of the prepared NF membranes were mainly concentrated at 2–4 nm. The highest proportion of pore sizes in M0-5 was 3.68 nm, while the highest proportion of pore sizes was decreased to 2.52 nm for M0-15 with the electrospray time prolonged from 5 to 15 min. In addition, when the GQDs were added to M0.1–15, the proportion of 2.68 nm pores was much higher than that of 3.56 nm pores.

The zeta potential played a vital role in membrane separation, particularly in the separation of high valence anions. Fig. 6 b and c showed the zeta potential of PA/PVC and GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes, respectively. At a pH of 7, all NF membranes exhibited negative zeta potential. As mentioned before, the monomer content deposited onto the substrate surface increased with an increase in the electrospray time, resulting in an increased surface electronegativity (-6 to −15.4) for the PA/PVC NF membranes (Fig. 6 b). As shown in Fig. 6 c, the surface electronegativity of the GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes was further increased (−6.1 to −25.5) when GQDs were incorporated into the membrane because of the rich carboxyl groups at the edges of GQDs [30,38]. Consequently, the synergistic effects of the high surface electronegativity and small pore size led to GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes with good rejection performance, which would be discussed later in details.

Fig. 6 d and e demonstrated the instantaneous water contact angle when water droplets just contacted on the surface of the NF membranes. All the PA/PVC and GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes showed good hydrophilicity, with water contact angles being less than 60°. The decrease in the contact angles (55.7°–35.0°) for PA/PVC NF membranes (Fig. 6 d) was due to structural changes in the membrane surface with increasing electrospray time, which heled make the PA layer a defect-free while increasing the surface roughness (Fig. 8
,
Fig. S7 ). The smaller contact angles (40.2°–27.5°) for GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes (Fig. 6 e) were due to the minor increased roughness ( Fig. S7 ), and the introduction of carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups by GQDs (Fig. 3 d) which might be exposed on the PA nanofilm [40,49]. What’s more, the water droplets contacted on the membrane surface after 10 s were showed the same trend as above ( Fig. S5 ).

Fig. 7 and Fig. S6 showed the cross-sectional morphologies of the prepared PA/PVC NF membranes. As the electrospray time prolonging from 5 to 40 min, the thickness of the resulting PA nanofilm increased from 16.3 ± 2.1 to 113.6 ± 3.5 nm, corresponding to a growth rate of 2.84 nm/min. This result demonstrated that the thickness of the PA nanofilms could be controlled readily by fine tuning of the electrospray time during the E-IP process.

Fig. 8 showed the surface morphologies of the PA/PVC NF membranes at different electrospray time. On the surface of M0-5, M0-10 and M0-15 membranes (with electrospray time of 5, 10 and 15 min, respectively), the images of the pores of the underneath substrate were noticeable, which indicated that the PA nanofilms formed on the surface of the PVC substrate were very thin that was unable to completely block electron beam passage. However, these three membranes showed relatively smooth surfaces with no obvious agglomerates or ridge-valley structures, which may be attributed to the interfacial reaction between PIP and TMC microdroplets with stable interfaces and a high interfacial tension. On the other hand, with a further increase in the electrospray time, the surface structures of the NF membranes would change significantly, which was consistent with their AFM images from Fig. S7 . For instance, the M0-20 membrane showed agglomerates on the surface, while the M0-30 and M0-40 showed irregularly distributed clusters and ridge-valley structures, respectively. Such changes in surface morphologies were mainly due to co-deposition of an increasingly larger amounts of reacting oil- and water-based microdroplets, which gradually destroyed their interfacial stabilities [50]. These results were almost consistent with that those reported prior in the literature [24].

The filtration performance of the GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes was evaluated using aqueous feed solutions (concentration 2 g/L) of Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2 and NaCl at 0.6 MPa operating pressure, and the results were shown in Fig. 10 . As shown in Fig. 10 a, with an increase in the electrospray time, the water flux tended to decrease, whereas the Na2SO4 rejection of M0 and M0.05 membranes gradually increased and stabilized at about 96 % at an electrospray time of 30 min. Similar trends were also observed in previous studies [32]. On the one hand, increasing the electrospray time increased the coverage of the PA nanofilm on the substrate membrane and a defect-free separation layer was formed with the electrospray time long enough. In the meanwhile, the PA nanofilm was increasingly crosslinked (see Fig. 5 a). Therefore, the membrane rejection rate increased while the permeation flux decreased.

It may be noticed that the Na2SO4 rejection of M0.1 and M0.5 membranes initially increased with the prolonged electrospray time and then decreased slightly. At the early stage of the electrospray process, by the addition of a small quantity of GQDs (concentration: 0.1 g/L, 0.5 g/L; electrospray time: 10 min) led to an obvious increase in the degree of crosslinking of the PA nanofilm (Fig. 5 b), which might result in a rapid increase in Na2SO4 rejection. With a further increase in the electrospray time (after 20 min), large amounts of GQDs owned hydrogen bonds, electrostatic attraction and steric hindrance to PIP in aqueous microdroplets, would significantly decrease the diffusion rate of PIP monomer to the microdroplets interface and depressed the reaction between PIP and TMC [29], the PA layer was trending to form a layered structure ( Fig. S8 ), which reduced the integrity and compactness of the PA layer and resulted in a lower Na2SO4 rejection (i.e., M0.5-40 membrane).

Fig. 10 c and d showed the Na2SO4 rejection and permeation flux of the as-prepared NF membranes with different GQDs concentrations at an electrospray time of 15 and 20 min, respectively. With the increase in GQDs concentration, both the Na2SO4 rejection and permeation flux of the NF membranes tended to increase and then slightly decrease. At an electrospray time of 15 min, the membrane (i.e., M0.1-15 membrane) exhibited a high Na2SO4 rejection of 98.8 % with a permeation flux of 41.0 L/(m2·h), while increasing the electrospray time to 20 min (i.e., M0.1-20 membrane) resulted in a Na2SO4 rejection of 97.1 % and a permeation flux of 27.2 L/(m2·h). In Fig. 10 e and f, under the short electrospray time (i.e., 2, 4 and 5 min), the NF membranes performed a low Na2SO4 rejection, but exhibited a relatively high CR rejection (over 98 %), and the addition of GQDs had little raised the permeation flux at such a short electrospray time.

Based on the high Na2SO4 rejection and the middle electrospray time (i.e., 15, 20 min) required to form the NF membrane, additional GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes under different conditions were fabricated and tested for NF performance toward different salts, and the results were shown in Fig. 10 b. The order of salt rejection was Na2SO4 > MgSO4 > MgCl2 > NaCl as expected based on the synergistic effects of Donnan exclusion and size sieving of the PA nanofilm [51]. In addition, these membranes showed a clear distinction between Na2SO4 and NaCl rejections. It appeared to indicate that the high electronegativity on the membrane surface provided a greater repulsion to divalent anions than monovalent anions, and SO4 2- had a greater resistance to passing through the membranes than Cl-. The short-term stabilities of M0.1-15 and M0.5-15 membranes were demonstrated in Fig. 11 , which indicated the GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes with good stability in water treatment.

A crossflow filtration system was used to conduct a circulating experiment to further evaluate the antifouling behavior of the membranes. The feed solution used here was a mixture of 2.0 g/L of Na2SO4 and 0.1 g/L of CR solution. After the membrane was operated in 60 min at 6 bar operating pressure, the contaminated membrane was cleaned with pure water at 6 bar for 30 min before the next cycle test. And the circulating dye desalination test results of the M0.1-2 and M0.1-4 were shown in Fig. 12 . It can be seen that both of these two membranes revealed a stable rejection rate for CR (>98.5 %) and Na2SO4 (<50.0 %), and the permeation flux of these two membranes decreased slightly after 4 cycles. In addition, the permeation flux recovery ratio in each circle test was over 90 % after every pure water cleaning for these two membranes, which indicated these two membranes have excellent antifouling properties. Thus, the membrane under a short electrospray time (i.e., 2, 4 min) could be used for the practical application of dye desalination.

Fig. 13
a showed the comparison of the NaCl/Na2SO4 separation performance of GQDs-PA/PVC NF membrane with various other E-IP membranes reported in the literature [18,19,32,33,48,52,53,65]. It can be seen from Fig. 13 a that the M0.1-15 membrane presented a highest NaCl/Na2SO4 selectivity, indicating the advantage of GQDs-PA/PVC NF membrane in the selective separation of NaCl/Na2SO4. Moreover, Fig. 13 b showed the comparison of the dye desalination performance of GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes at short electrospray times with other dye desalination membranes reported in the literature [17,54–62], which demonstrated the GQDs-PA/PVC NF membranes (M0.1-4, M0.1-5) possessing a high salt/dye selectivity and good permeation flux. Although E-IP method has certain advantages in PA nanofilm thickness controllability and nanomaterials dispersion, the comprehensive performance of the PA nanofilm obtained by E-IP method still needs to be further improved compared with the NF membranes prepared by other methods [4,9,14,29,61–64].

Ultrathin PA nanofilms with controllable structures were obtained via E-IP using GQDs as nanofillers. It was shown that the physicochemical structures of the PA nanofilms could be controlled by the adjusting the electrospray duration and GQDs concentrations in the aqueous monomer solution. Incorporating an appropriate amount of GQDs in the PA nanofilm would promote the crosslinking reaction at the microdroplet interface and could significantly reduce the electrospray time required (i.e., 30 to 15 min) for the resulting membrane to achieve a Na2SO4 rejection of over 95 %, with a significantly reduced skin layer thickness in the PA nanofilms and improved the permeation flux. And under a short electrospray time (i.e., 2–5 min), the NF membranes with the high salt/dye selectivity could be used for the practical application of dye desalination. This study not only reveals an idea for the uniform addition of nanomaterials into the PA nanofilm, but also provides an important strategy for the structural adjustment of the PA nanofilm, which has a crucial potential in the field of seawater desalination and dye desalination.

Yumin Sun: Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Hailiang Liu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Yang Qin: Methodology. Xianshe Feng: Writing – review & editing. Junyan Chen: Investigation. Qinglin Huang: Software. Changfa Xiao: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the research funding provided by the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by China Association for Science and Technology (YESS20200231), the financial support from the China Scholarship Council (202008120087). We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University for the test analysis work.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2023.123831.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
